content.text: Non-functional requirements (NFRs) for latency and throughput define the performance envelope within which an agentic AI system must operate. Unlike functional requirements that specify what the system does, these NFRs specify how fast and how much the system can do it—constraints that are invisible when the system is healthy but catastrophic when violated.

In agentic pipelines, latency and throughput behave differently than in conventional web applications. A typical agentic pipeline strings together multiple dependent steps: input preprocessing, retrieval (RAG), one or more LLM inference calls, tool dispatches, and output formatting. Each step contributes its own latency, and those latencies compound. A pipeline with five sequential stages each adding 500 ms delivers a 2.5-second response even if each individual component appears fast in isolation.

Throughput adds a second dimension. The number of requests per second a pipeline can sustain is bounded by the slowest stage—typically LLM inference—which operates under hard rate limits imposed by the model provider. Simultaneously, throughput can be measured in tokens per second: the rate at which the system generates model output, which directly governs the cost per request and the maximum concurrent user capacity.

Agentic systems are frequently deployed in user-facing applications where latency directly affects satisfaction and task completion rates. Research on web application performance consistently shows that response times above one second cause users to lose focus, and above ten seconds cause abandonment. Multi-step agent pipelines are at particular risk because each additional LLM inference call adds 500 ms to 3 seconds of irreducible latency, even at optimal model serving infrastructure.

Throughput NFRs matter for cost control as much as capacity. LLM inference is priced per token, and an agent that uses 10,000 input tokens and produces 2,000 output tokens per request costs 40 times more than one that uses 250 input and 50 output tokens. Without throughput NFRs that specify token budgets, agent pipelines can silently balloon in cost when deployed at scale. A pipeline handling 10,000 requests per day with an unconstrained token budget may spend ten times more than the product team expected.

Latency and throughput NFRs are also the foundation for capacity planning, load testing, and SLA commitments. Without concrete targets, engineering teams cannot determine whether observed performance is acceptable, cannot design meaningful load tests, and cannot make commitments to customers about response times.

A defined P99 latency target gives the engineering team an unambiguous pass/fail criterion for performance tests. There is no debate about whether 4.2 seconds is acceptable when the NFR says P99 must be below 3.0 seconds.
Throughput NFRs (requests per second, tokens per second) translate directly into compute, concurrency, and API quota requirements. Infrastructure can be sized before launch.
Token budget NFRs cap per-request and per-day token consumption, making operational cost predictable and preventing runaway spending on long-context LLM calls.
Latency P50/P95/P99 targets form the technical basis for customer-facing SLA commitments. The engineering team cannot commit to a 2-second SLA without measuring against that target continuously.
Maximum queue depth NFRs define the back-pressure threshold at which the system should start rejecting or prioritising requests, preventing cascade failures.
Concrete throughput targets define the exact test load to simulate. Without them, load tests are arbitrary and their results are uninterpretable.

Aggressive latency targets are expensive. Achieving P99 below 2 seconds for a multi-step agent pipeline typically requires parallel execution of independent steps, model caching, and pre-computation of common retrievals—each of which adds architectural complexity and cost. The engineering investment required to move from P99 of 4 seconds to P99 of 2 seconds can be three times the effort needed to achieve P99 of 6 seconds.

Throughput scaling conflicts with latency optimization. Batching multiple requests together improves GPU utilisation and increases token throughput, but it increases latency for individual requests waiting for the batch to fill. Choosing between latency-optimised (low batch size) and throughput-optimised (large batch size) serving is a fundamental trade-off that the NFRs must explicitly address.

Restricting max_tokens forces the model to truncate outputs. If the token budget is too tight, the agent begins returning incomplete answers or calling tools insufficiently, degrading functional correctness for the sake of cost.

Measuring and enforcing P99 latency requires distributed tracing infrastructure. Teams that lack tracing may default to average latency, which is misleading because it masks the tail latency that affects a non-trivial fraction of real users.
Identifying which pipeline stage drives high P99 requires per-stage instrumentation. Without stage-level breakdowns, latency budgets cannot be allocated effectively.
A latency budget allocates the total allowed end-to-end latency across each stage of the pipeline. If the product requires P95 end-to-end latency below 5 seconds, that budget is distributed:

attrs.latex: L_{e2e} = L_{parse} + L_{retrieval} + L_{inference} \cdot N_{turns} + L_{tool} \cdot N_{tools} + L_{format}

content.text: is the expected number of LLM inference rounds and
is the expected number of tool calls per pipeline run. For a two-turn pipeline with one tool call:

This leaves 1,900 ms of headroom for P95 overshoot. The budget for each stage becomes the target that teams own: the retrieval team owns the 200 ms target; the LLM integration team owns the 1,200 ms per-turn target.

P50 (median) latency describes the experience of the majority of users. P95 describes the experience of 1 in 20 users—a non-trivial tail in a high-volume system. P99 describes 1 in 100 users. Choosing which percentiles to specify in NFRs depends on the SLA tier: a premium enterprise customer SLA typically targets P99, while a consumer product may target P95 for cost reasons.

The spread between P50 and P99 is itself a signal. A ratio above 5x (P99 more than five times P50) indicates high variance—often caused by occasional cold-start latency, cache misses, or token-budget throttling. High variance is often harder to fix than uniformly high latency because the root cause is intermittent.
Throughput in agentic systems has two distinct metrics. Requests per second (RPS) measures pipeline invocations. Tokens per second (TPS) measures model output generation rate. The relationship is:

is the mean output token count per request. A pipeline serving 50 RPS with a mean of 300 output tokens per request generates 15,000 output tokens per second, which must fit within the model provider's rate limit.

attrs.alt: Latency budget architecture: User Request flows through four pipeline stages (Input Parsing 20ms P50, Retrieval 150ms P50, LLM Inference 1200ms P50, Tool Dispatch 300ms P50), all feeding into Throughput Monitoring (RPS, tokens/s, queue depth), which connects to a Token Budget Controller.

content.text: The diagram shows the latency budget allocated across four pipeline stages. LLM Inference dominates at 1,200 ms P50—over 70% of the budget in a typical two-stage pipeline—making it the primary target for optimization. The Throughput Monitoring layer aggregates stage-level telemetry into three signals: requests per second, tokens per second, and queue depth. The Token Budget Controller enforces per-request and per-day token quotas, firing alerts at 80% consumption and hard-stopping at 100%.

Latency and throughput NFRs should be defined before the first production deployment of any agent pipeline. They are most critical for user-facing applications (interactive chat, document review tools) and batch pipelines with downstream SLAs. They are less urgent for internal research or evaluation pipelines where a human is not waiting for a response.
Define P50 and P95 for interactive applications
where users are waiting synchronously. P99 matters if the tail affects a significant absolute number of users.
Define token budgets before enabling free-tier access
to any pipeline. Without a hard cap, a single malicious or misconfigured client can exhaust a daily token quota in minutes.
Define queue depth limits before load testing
to know when the system is saturated versus when it is still within its operating envelope.
Revisit NFRs at each model change.
Upgrading to a more capable model may increase per-request latency and token cost even if the response is better, requiring budget reallocation.
Instrument every pipeline stage with distributed tracing. Record start and end timestamps for each stage; compute per-stage latency histograms.
Define per-stage latency budgets in the pipeline configuration file. Enforce them as soft warnings at 80% and hard SLO violations at 100%.
Implement token budget enforcement in the LLM call wrapper: set max_tokens per request, track cumulative daily tokens per pipeline ID, and reject or throttle requests that exceed the daily quota.
Configure queue depth monitoring on the task queue. Alert when depth exceeds the NFR threshold; implement back-pressure by returning 429 responses when the queue is full.
Write load tests that drive the pipeline to the throughput target and verify that latency percentiles remain within NFR bounds under that load.
Add NFR assertions to the CI pipeline: run a subset of load tests on every deployment and fail the build if P95 latency exceeds the target.

# nfrs/latency-throughput.yaml
# Non-functional requirements for the document-review agentic pipeline

latency:
  measurement: end_to_end_wall_clock
  percentiles:
    p50_ms: 1800
    p95_ms: 4500
    p99_ms: 7000
  per_stage:
    input_parsing:
      p99_ms: 80
    retrieval_rag:
      p99_ms: 500
    llm_inference_per_turn:
      p99_ms: 4000
    tool_dispatch_per_call:
      p99_ms: 800
    output_formatting:
      p99_ms: 90
  evaluation_window: rolling_5_minutes
  breach_policy: alert_on_p95_breach, page_on_p99_breach

throughput:
  target_rps: 50
  burst_rps: 120
  max_queue_depth: 100
  queue_depth_alert_threshold: 80
  back_pressure_policy: return_429_when_queue_full

token_budget:
  per_request:
    max_input_tokens: 8192
    max_output_tokens: 2048
    max_total_tokens: 10240
  per_pipeline_per_day:
    limit_tokens: 50_000_000
    alert_at_pct: 80
    hard_stop_at_pct: 100
  cost_cap_usd_per_day: 500.00

load_testing:
  framework: locust
  ramp_up_seconds: 60
  sustained_load_seconds: 300
  target_rps: 50
  pass_criteria:
    p95_latency_ms: 4500
    p99_latency_ms: 7000
    error_rate_pct: 0.1

A customer support platform deploys an agentic triage agent that classifies incoming tickets, retrieves relevant knowledge base articles, and drafts a response suggestion for human agents. The product SLA commits to displaying a draft suggestion within 6 seconds of ticket submission. The team decomposed this into a pipeline budget: 30 ms for input classification, 200 ms for knowledge base retrieval, 2,500 ms for LLM response drafting, and 50 ms for formatting—totalling 2,780 ms P50, leaving 3,220 ms headroom for P95/P99 variance.

At launch, P99 latency measured 8.2 seconds—exceeding the 6-second SLA. Distributed tracing revealed that 4,800 ms of the P99 tail was consumed by a retrieval step that occasionally issued a full-text search against a poorly indexed knowledge base collection. After adding a vector index and caching the top-50 article embeddings at startup, the P99 dropped to 5.8 seconds. Token budget tracking showed average per-request token usage of 6,100 tokens—well under the 10,240 limit—but revealed that 3% of requests (long ticket threads) consumed over 9,000 tokens. A context-trimming step that summarized ticket history reduced mean token usage to 4,200 and P99 latency to 4.9 seconds.

Average latency is mathematically misleading in skewed distributions. A pipeline with 95% of requests completing in 1 second and 5% taking 10 seconds has an average of 1.45 seconds—which looks acceptable but conceals the fact that 5% of users experience 10-second waits. Always define and measure P95 and P99, not mean.

Testing each pipeline stage in isolation is insufficient. Stage latencies under production conditions differ from isolated tests because of queue contention, context switching, and shared resource competition. Load tests must drive the full end-to-end pipeline at target RPS to observe true percentile distributions.

Token budgets set on mean input size underestimate tail behaviour. If the mean input is 2,000 tokens but the 99th percentile is 8,000 tokens, a budget set to the mean will frequently be breached in production. Set input token limits based on P99 observed usage, not the mean.

A pipeline without a queue depth limit under overload simply keeps accepting requests, building an unbounded backlog. Each queued request holds memory and eventually times out before it is ever processed, producing a 100% error rate for users who submitted during the peak. Define the queue depth NFR and enforce it with 429 responses so callers can retry rather than silently timing out.
Define budgets per stage, not just end-to-end.
Stage-level budgets create ownership: the retrieval team owns the retrieval budget, the inference team owns the LLM budget.
Server-side latency measurements miss network time and serialization overhead. Measure end-to-end latency from the perspective of the calling client, not just the service.
Use rolling windows for SLO evaluation.
A 5-minute rolling window smooths instantaneous spikes without hiding sustained degradation. Avoid evaluating SLOs on single-request measurements.
Instead of a hard per-request limit, allow a per-request maximum with a daily quota. Short requests use fewer tokens, leaving more budget for the occasional long request.
Run a lightweight load test on every deployment—even at 10 RPS for 60 seconds—and fail the build if P95 exceeds the NFR. Catch regressions before production.

Use tracing data to identify the dominant latency stage before investing in optimization. LLM inference is usually the bottleneck, but retrieval, serialization, and cold-start can dominate in specific configurations.
The NFR must specify how percentiles are computed (rolling window duration, minimum sample size) to make SLO reporting reproducible across teams.

The primary scalability constraint for agentic pipelines is the model provider's rate limit, measured in requests per minute (RPM) and tokens per minute (TPM). A pipeline limited to 100,000 TPM with a mean of 5,000 tokens per request can sustain at most 20 requests per minute (0.33 RPS) without exceeding the rate limit. Scaling beyond this requires multiple API keys, multiple model providers, or model self-hosting.

Horizontal scaling of the pipeline itself (more workers) increases throughput up to the model rate limit but does not reduce per-request latency. Latency reduction requires pipeline restructuring: parallelizing independent stages, caching retrievals, reducing context length, or switching to a faster model. These are architectural changes, not infrastructure scaling changes.
If retrieval and input classification are independent, execute them concurrently. In Python, use
to run multiple awaitable stages simultaneously, reducing additive latency to the maximum of the parallel stages.
For pipelines with predictable prompt templates, precompute the KV cache for the static portion of the prompt. This reduces effective TTFT (time to first token) by up to 40% for long system prompts.

Route simple requests to a faster, cheaper model and complex requests to a more capable model. A classifier that decides routing adds 50 ms but can save 1,500 ms on the 60% of requests that qualify for the fast path.
The standard for distributed tracing. Instrument pipeline stages with
to capture per-stage latency. Exporters send spans to Jaeger, Honeycomb, or Datadog.
metric type) and queue depth gauges. Grafana dashboards visualise P50/P95/P99 over rolling windows and fire alerts when thresholds are breached.
Python load testing framework. Define user behaviour as Python classes; Locust distributes the load across multiple workers and reports latency histograms at the target RPS.
All major model providers (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) provide token usage dashboards and rate limit monitoring. Subscribe to usage alerts at 80% of daily TPM quota.
JavaScript-based load testing tool. Integrates with CI pipelines and provides built-in threshold assertions (e.g., 'fail if P95 > 4500 ms') that map directly to NFR targets.

The combination of per-percentile latency budgets, token budgets, and queue depth limits provides the most complete coverage for production agentic systems. Average latency alone is insufficient for SLA commitments. Token budgets without latency targets miss the user experience dimension. Full NFR coverage is most valuable when the system serves external users or operates under a cost constraint.

Latency and throughput NFRs interact directly with reliability. A pipeline that consistently operates near its throughput limit has little headroom to absorb traffic spikes, making it fragile. NFRs should specify a burst capacity target (typically 2–3x the sustained RPS target) that the system must handle without SLO breach.
Token quota exhaustion as a denial of service vector.
An authenticated user who submits many large-context requests can exhaust a shared daily token quota, degrading service for other users. Per-user and per-organisation token budgets prevent this.
Rising P99 latency often precedes error rate increases. Configure latency-based alerting at a threshold below the SLO breach point to give operators time to respond before customers are affected.
Queue depth as a circuit breaker signal.

When queue depth reaches the NFR threshold, the system should return 429 responses with Retry-After headers rather than accepting additional requests into an unbounded backlog. This prevents memory exhaustion and makes the overload visible to callers.

Define an NFR for maximum acceptable latency increase during a model provider degradation event. Implement fallback routing to an alternative model when P99 latency exceeds twice the normal NFR target for more than 60 seconds.
Define latency NFRs as P50/P95/P99 budgets allocated per pipeline stage, not as average end-to-end targets—stage-level budgets create clear ownership and enable precise optimisation.
LLM inference is the dominant latency contributor in most agentic pipelines; optimise it first through context reduction, model tiering, and KV cache prefilling before tuning other stages.
Throughput has two dimensions: requests per second (capacity) and tokens per second (cost); both must be measured and bounded by NFRs for production systems.
Token budget NFRs are a cost-control mechanism: set per-request and daily limits, alert at 80%, and hard-stop at 100% to prevent runaway spending.
Queue depth limits define the back-pressure point; enforce them with 429 responses rather than accepting requests into an unbounded backlog.
Load tests must exercise the full end-to-end pipeline at target RPS; stage-level isolation tests miss queue contention and shared resource effects.
Add latency assertions to CI as lightweight gating tests so latency regressions are caught before they reach production.
